source_intake_record.raw_question_text:
A $5 \times 5$ square is covered with $1 \times 1$ tiles. The design on each tile is made up of three dark triangles and one light triangle (see diagram). The triangles of neighbouring tiles always have the same colour where they join along an edge. The border of the large square is made of dark and light triangles. What is the smallest number of dark triangles that could be amongst them?
<image1>

image_dependency: The per-tile triangle arrangement is not fully specified in text; solving depends on the visual design/orientation of the tile
Reasoning involves neighbouring tile edge matches and border triangles, which are strongly diagram-dependent
multi_step: The task combines local adjacency constraints ('neighbouring tiles always have the same colour where they join') with a global objective ('smallest number' on the border)
It is an optimization problem over a 5x5 arrangement, which suggests more than one reasoning step
The solver likely must propagate colour/placement constraints across multiple tiles and then count or minimize boundary dark triangles
Metadata indicates aggressive multi-solution handling, consistent with nontrivial reasoning potential
verifiability: answer_type is numeric and answer_text is a single value ('5')
registry_passed=true, question and answer are both present
A proposed solution can in principle be checked by validating edge-colour consistency and counting border dark triangles
Verification is somewhat harder than simple arithmetic because the visual tile pattern is required

decision_reasons: question, image, and numeric answer are all present, so the core semantics are intact
registry passed with no recorded asset issues
high image dependency is acceptable here because the required diagram asset exists
verifiability is strong because the target answer is a single numeric value
multi-step and optimization characteristics make it a valuable candidate despite moderate reasoning complexity
